title: Check a finite-precision arithmetic problem
summary: Use the calculator to compare an exact expression with a machine-computed result.

steps.title: Enter the expression or numbers
steps.description: Provide the arithmetic problem you want to inspect.
steps.outcome: The calculator has the full problem setup.

steps.title: Choose precision settings
steps.description: Select the number of significant digits and whether to chop or round.

steps.title: Compute and compare
steps.description: Review the machine result alongside the exact or reference value.
steps.outcome: You can see how finite precision changes the answer.

title: Evaluate approximation quality
summary: Use error metrics to judge whether an approximation is acceptable.

steps.title: Provide the true and approximate values
steps.description: Enter the reference value and the approximation you want to assess.
steps.outcome: The calculator has the pair needed for comparison.

steps.title: Read the reported metrics
steps.description: Inspect absolute error, relative error, and significant-digit style results.
steps.outcome: You have quantitative measures of approximation quality.

steps.title: Interpret the result
steps.description: Use the explanations to decide how trustworthy the approximation is.
steps.outcome: You can explain the approximation in numerical-analysis terms.

title: Compare polynomial evaluation methods
summary: Study how Horner and direct evaluation behave on the same polynomial.

steps.title: Enter the polynomial, x-value, and precision settings
steps.description: Set up the polynomial problem you want to evaluate, then choose significant digits and the machine rule.
steps.outcome: Both methods will work from the same mathematical input and finite-precision setup.

steps.title: Compute the comparison
steps.description: Run the polynomial module to produce the reference, Horner, and direct results under the selected precision settings.
steps.outcome: You get multiple evaluation paths to compare in the same machine environment.

steps.title: Inspect method differences
steps.description: Use the outputs to discuss operation ordering and sensitivity.
steps.outcome: You can explain why equivalent forms may behave differently numerically.

title: Approximate a root with an iterative method
summary: Use one of the root-finding methods to study approximate solutions and stopping behavior.

steps.title: Choose a method and provide starting data
steps.description: Select Bisection, Newton-Raphson, Secant, False Position, or Fixed Point, then enter the function, initial values, and precision settings required by the machine approximation.
steps.outcome: The method has the function, starting setup, and finite-precision context it needs.

steps.title: Set stopping controls
steps.description: Choose a stopping mode and value that fit the exercise.
steps.outcome: The iteration has a clear stopping rule.

steps.title: Run and interpret the approximation
steps.description: Review the approximate root and related method output.
steps.outcome: You can discuss convergence and the meaning of the reported answer.
